comment_text.sn7.3:1.2: His name or epithet asurindaka (“demon-lord”) is sometimes used of Vṛtra, and hence is the exact opposite of what a brahmin should be.

root_text.sn7.3:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā rājagahe viharati veḷuvane kalandakanivāpe.
root_text.sn7.3:1.3: “bhāradvājagotto brāhmaṇo kira samaṇassa gotamassa santike agārasmā anagāriyaṁ pabbajito”ti kupito anattamano yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ asabbhāhi pharusāhi vācāhi akkosati paribhāsati.
root_text.sn7.3:1.5: Atha kho asurindakabhāradvājo brāhmaṇo bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn7.3:6.1: Evaṁ vutte, asurindakabhāradvājo brāhmaṇo bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn7.3:6.4: Aññataro ca panāyasmā bhāradvājo arahataṁ ahosīti.

translation_text.sn7.3:1.1: At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground.
translation_text.sn7.3:1.2: The brahmin Bhāradvāja the Fiend heard a rumor to the effect that
translation_text.sn7.3:1.3: a brahmin of the Bhāradvāja clan had gone forth from the lay life to homelessness in the presence of the ascetic Gotama. Angry and displeased he went to the Buddha and abused and insulted him with rude, harsh words.
translation_text.sn7.3:1.4: But when he said this, the Buddha kept silent.
translation_text.sn7.3:1.5: Then Bhāradvāja the Fiend said to the Buddha,
translation_text.sn7.3:3.1: When you get angry at an angry person
translation_text.sn7.3:3.2: you just make things worse for yourself.
translation_text.sn7.3:3.3: When you don’t get angry at an angry person
translation_text.sn7.3:3.4: you win a battle hard to win.
translation_text.sn7.3:4.1: When you know that the other is angry,
translation_text.sn7.3:4.2: you act for the good of both
translation_text.sn7.3:4.4: if you’re mindful and stay calm.
translation_text.sn7.3:6.1: When he had spoken, Bhāradvāja the Fiend said to the Buddha,
translation_text.sn7.3:6.4: And Venerable Bhāradvāja became one of the perfected.
